Same FHA insurance, same 3.5% down, applied to a home that doesn't exist yet. In {COUNTY} County — including around {CITY1} and {CITY2} — the same guideline applies.

q: Can I buy land now and build later with FHA in {COUNTY} County?
a: FHA's construction financing is built to do both at once — land and build in one closing. If you buy land separately first, that's fine too; your equity in it counts when you're ready to build. In {COUNTY} County — including around {CITY1} and {CITY2} — the same guideline applies.

q: Is rural land eligible for FHA construction in {COUNTY} County?
a: Yes — FHA has no rural restriction and no urban requirement. Acreage outside {SEAT} works as long as the site supports a residential appraisal and meets property standards. In {COUNTY} County — including around {CITY1} and {CITY2} — the same guideline applies.

q: Can I build in a rural part of {COUNTY} County with FHA?
a: Yes — FHA has no geographic restriction, so rural acreage outside {CITY1} works just as well as an infill lot in {SEAT}. If you're comparing against USDA for a rural site, we'll price both side by side.

q: Is there a maximum acreage for FHA in {COUNTY} County?
a: No hard acreage cap — the real test is whether the appraiser can find comparable sales supporting a residential (not agricultural) property. Large {COUNTY} County parcels routinely work when nearby comps exist.

q: How much acreage can I build on on a FHA construction loan in {COUNTY} County?
a: Manufactured home builds are capped at 10 acres. Site-built projects are evaluated on the appraisal — the property in {COUNTY} County needs to make sense as residential, and comparable sales need to support the value.

q: Can I build in a flood zone with FHA in {COUNTY} County?
a: Site-built homes can be financed in flood zones with proper flood insurance and elevation compliance. Manufactured homes are the exception — the dwelling itself can't sit in Flood Zone A or V, though the land can include those areas. In {COUNTY} County — including around {CITY1} and {CITY2} — the same guideline applies.

q: Can I build near the coast or in a low-lying area on a FHA construction loan in {COUNTY} County?
a: Flood zones and site elevation are reviewed as part of project approval, and very low-elevation sites may not qualify. If your lot in {COUNTY} County is coastal or low-lying, send us the address first — we'll check it before you spend a dollar on plans.

q: What road access does my building site need on a FHA construction loan in {COUNTY} County?
a: The property needs legal access — a public road or a recorded easement — passable year-round. Shared private roads work with a maintenance agreement, a common setup on rural {COUNTY} County lots.

q: What utilities does my site need for FHA in {COUNTY} County?
a: Safe water, sanitary waste disposal, and electricity — public hookups where practical, or private well and septic where they're not. Utility connection costs belong in the construction budget. In {COUNTY} County — including around {CITY1} and {CITY2} — the same guideline applies.

q: Can I build on land my family owns on a FHA construction loan in {COUNTY} County?
a: Yes — family land can be gifted or sold to you, and gift-of-equity rules often let that land value serve as your down payment. It's one of the most powerful paths to a {COUNTY} County build with minimal cash.

q: Does FHA allow leased land or land trusts in {COUNTY} County?
a: Standard programs require fee simple ownership — you own the dirt outright. Land trusts and most leased-land arrangements fall outside typical eligibility. In {COUNTY} County — including around {CITY1} and {CITY2} — the same guideline applies.

q: Are condos eligible for FHA in {COUNTY} County?
a: Yes, when the project in {COUNTY} County is FHA-approved — an approval that attaches to the whole development, not just your unit. We check the FHA condo list before you fall in love with a unit.

q: What are FHA's manufactured home requirements in {COUNTY} County?
a: Built after June 1976 to HUD code, at least 12 feet wide with 600+ square feet, permanently affixed on an engineer-certified foundation, on land you own, and taxed as real property. Multi-wide only — single-wides don't qualify. In {COUNTY} County — including around {CITY1} and {CITY2} — the same guideline applies.

q: What are FHA minimum property standards in {COUNTY} County?
a: Safety, soundness, and security — no structural defects, working systems, safe water, and no health hazards. New construction clears these by default; they matter most when comparing a new build against an aging resale. In {COUNTY} County — including around {CITY1} and {CITY2} — the same guideline applies.

q: What about buying a fixer-upper instead of building on a FHA construction loan in {COUNTY} County?
a: That's FHA 203(k), which wraps renovation costs into a purchase loan for an existing home. Ground-up new construction runs through One-Time Close instead — different tools for different projects. In {COUNTY} County — including around {CITY1} and {CITY2} — the same guideline applies.

q: Can I buy the land from a third party at closing on a FHA construction loan in {COUNTY} County?
a: Yes — the lot can be owned by you, the builder, or a bona fide third party, with a land contract separate from the builder's construction contract. Both close together in the single closing. In {COUNTY} County — including around {CITY1} and {CITY2} — the same guideline applies.

q: Are there restrictions on resale or deed-restricted communities on a FHA construction loan in {COUNTY} County?
a: Resale deed restrictions aren't permitted on this program. If your lot in {COUNTY} County sits in a community with unusual deed terms, flag it early and we'll review the specifics before you commit.
